text.title: Story Mapping Process: Five Steps
text.summary: Step-by-step process for building a story map, from framing the journey to slicing releases, with failure modes per step.

rule.statement: Before writing any activity, define: who is the user, what is their big goal, and where does the journey start and end. Input persona brief before mapping.
rule.rationale: Without persona context, agents and workshop participants produce generic backbones ("View", "Browse", "Manage") that fit nothing specific.

rule.statement: Build the backbone first, collaboratively, with at least one user-voice input (interview notes count). Get backbone approval before generating tasks — backbone errors propagate to every task under them.
rule.rationale: Skipping the backbone workshop and jumping to tasks is the number-one failure mode in story mapping.

antipattern.description: Backbone drifts toward implementation steps: "Authenticate user", "Query database".
antipattern.reason: Backbone activities must be user-observable life events, not system operations.

rule.statement: For each activity, write every task the user does there. Arrange vertically by priority. Include at least one error/recovery/abandonment path per column.
rule.rationale: Happy-path-only maps surface incomplete releases; error paths discovered post-launch cost more to fix.

rule.statement: Cap at 5 tasks per column before requiring justification. More than 5 per column signals the activity is too broad and should be split.
rule.rationale: Uncapped task generation produces 50+ cards; column density above 5 degrades the visual signal the map is meant to provide.

rule.statement: Draw a line after the single minimum task in each column. Verify the line crosses every column. Name the skeleton explicitly ("walking_skeleton") in any structured data format.
rule.rationale: An unnamed skeleton gets ignored during release planning. Explicit naming forces the team to acknowledge it as the MVP boundary.

rule.statement: Name each release with a value statement, not a version number. Validate: "Is this shippable to users as-is?" If no, the slice is too thin or incomplete.
rule.rationale: Version numbers carry no product meaning; value statements ("Users can search and buy") communicate scope to all stakeholders.

rule.statement: Store the map as structured data (JSON/YAML), not only as a whiteboard image. Agents and humans both need the map queryable.
rule.rationale: Whiteboard images cannot be updated from analytics data or referenced by downstream tools (backlog importers, sprint planners).

example.note: Release named "Customers can complete a purchase end-to-end" — value statement. Clear scope for all stakeholders.
Release named "v1.2" — carries no product meaning; team must read the task list to understand scope.
